verdict: 2026-06-25 23:02 PDT — The core store-adapter enumeration idea is sound and fits this repo: no new dependency is needed, `fs.rm(..., { force: true })` is the right primitive for idempotent file deletion, and Express middleware plus native `node:test` matches local convention. I would not build the sketch exactly as written, though: it currently couples a privacy delete path to rate-store crypto readiness, and it frames all uid-scoped data rights as a rates resource/module.

findings.title: DELETE is gated on rate-store crypto readiness
findings.claim: The sketch wraps DELETE in the existing `requirePresenter`, whose current implementation returns 503 when `RATE_STORE_KEY` is missing before it even resolves `uid`. Deleting the encrypted rate file does not require decrypting it, so this couples identity verification to read/write store readiness and creates a data-retention failure mode during key loss or key misconfiguration.
findings.alternative: Split the middleware shape: `requirePresenter` should only require Zoom client id/secret and resolve `req.uid`; a separate `requireRateStoreConfigured` should guard GET/PUT/export paths that need crypto. Let DELETE use identity-only auth plus `purgeUser(req.uid)`, while still returning 503 when Zoom identity config itself is absent.
findings.win: Deletion remains available in the exact failure mode where decrypt/export cannot work, and future privacy/webhook delete paths can reuse the same purge primitive without copying the store-readiness check.

findings.title: Account-scoped data rights are framed as rates
findings.claim: The sketch says `purgeUser`/`exportUser` are the single enumeration point for everything stored about a `uid`, but places that account-scoped registry in `rateStore.js` and exposes the destructive endpoint as `DELETE /api/rates`. That is internally consistent only while rates are the sole store; once entitlements, subscriptions, or summaries arrive, a rates module and rates route will own/delete unrelated account data.
findings.alternative: Use a neutral data-rights/user-data registry module for the single enumeration point, with rateStore as one adapter. Prefer account/data-rights routes such as `GET /api/me/export` and `DELETE /api/me/data`; keep `/api/rates` for the rate config resource if needed.
findings.win: The single enumeration point remains real without making future stores depend on the rates module, and the API name matches the destructive blast radius before client UI/docs make the route harder to rename.
